coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots assisted thermally activated delayed fluorescence to achieve stronger luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jian
Chen, Ling
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) feature a strongly cross-linked system with a large number of energy levels, which limits the vibration and rotation of the luminous center, reduces non-radiative relaxation deactivation, and allows for cross-linking-enhanced emission (CEE). This study first predicted the rationality of designing thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) molecules using theoretical calculations of electronic structure and interaction region indicators (IRI), and then introduced a highly cross-linked system of carbonized polymer dots to stabilize the triplet state of TADF and confine the vibrational and rotational relaxation. Finally, it is demonstrated through experiments, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) analysis, and electron density difference maps that the luminescence mechanism between CPDs and TADF is due to the formation of O–H⋯N hydrogen bond interactions, which promote the reverse intersystem crossing process of thermally activated delayed fluorescence, which achieves high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) in powder state and solves the problem of TADF stability. The PLQY of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs is 34.40 %, and the PLQY of Ph-DPCz@CPDs is 30.57 %. The produced TADF molecules and TADF@CPDs were used to detect electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signals, demonstrating the multifunctionality of TADF materials and proposing a specific notion for material design in biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Carbonized polymer dots
Hydrogen bond interactions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: From left to right: the optimized ground state conformation. HOMO (top) and LUMO (bottom) distributions. The natural transition orbital distribution diagram of the S1 state (the top is HONTO, the bottom is LUNTO). The natural transition of the T1 state orbital distribution map (the upper is HONTO, the lower is LUNTO). Theoretical simulation of S1 and T1 state energy levels.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra, (b) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz in powder. (c) Photoluminescence emission spectra, (d) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz in toluene solution.
UV–Vis absorption spectra for (a) Ph-tBuCz@CPDs, (b) Ph-DPCz@CPDs. X-ray diffraction for (c) Ph-tBuCz@CPDs, (d) Ph-DPCz@CPDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of Ph-DPCz@CPDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CPDs.

(a) ECL annihilation of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-tBuCz@CPDs in PBS (7.43) solution. (b) Cyclic voltammogram of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-tBuCz@CPDs in PBS (7.43) solution. (c) ECL annihilation of Ph-DPCz and Ph-DPCz@CPDs in PBS (7.43) solution. (d) Cyclic voltammogram of Ph-DPCz and Ph-DPCz@CPDs in PBS (7.43) solution. Inset: the annihilation ECL stability change of 25 circles of the material. Scan rate: 100 mV s−1.

(a) Electrostatic potential penetration map and (b) electron density difference map corresponding to Ph-tBuCz@CPDs. (c) Electrostatic potential penetration map and (d) electron density difference map corresponding to Ph-DPCz@CPDs. (e) Molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) values on the van der Waals surface, and green and yellow spheres represent local minima and maxima points, respectively.
(a) Ph-tBuCz@CPDs, (b) Ph-DPCz@CPDs in the full pH (pH: 2–14) range of pH-PL spectra. (c) Ph-tBuCz@CPD and Ph-DPCz@CPDs pH-fluorescence emission peak position trend graph in the full pH range.
The synthetic route of (a) Ph-tBuCz, Ph-DPCz and (b) CPDs.
Summary of related photophysical properties data.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Transient photoluminescence prompt life and delayed life.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Photoluminescence quantum yield for Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz in powder, toluene solution, Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots assisted thermally activated delayed fluorescence to achieve stronger luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) feature a strongly cross-linked system with a large number of energy levels, which limits the vibration and rotation of the luminous center, reduces non-radiative relaxation deactivation, and allows for cross-linking-enhanced emission (CEE). This study first predicted the rationality of designing thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) molecules using theoretical calculations of electronic structure and interaction region indicators (IRI), and then introduced a highly cross-linked system of carbonized polymer dots to stabilize the triplet state of TADF and confine the vibrational and rotational relaxation. Finally, it is demonstrated through experiments, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) analysis, and electron density difference maps that the luminescence mechanism between CPDs and TADF is due to the formation of O–H⋯N hydrogen bond interactions, which promote the reverse intersystem crossing process of thermally activated delayed fluorescence, which achieves high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) in powder state and solves the problem of TADF stability. The PLQY of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs is 34.40 %, and the PLQY of Ph-DPCz@CPDs is 30.57 %. The produced TADF molecules and TADF@CPDs were used to detect electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signals, demonstrating the multifunctionality of TADF materials and proposing a specific notion for material design in biosensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) guests have sparked a wave of research in the field of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) in recent years. The spin-orbit principle states that the lowest singlet excited state (S1) and lowest triplet excited state species (T1) formed by ion-ion annihilation are 25 % and 75 %, respectively [1,2]. Because of the presence of non-radiative transitions between the T1 and S0 states in traditional fluorescent molecular materials, the T1 state cannot effectively participate in the radiation process, and 100 % of the excitons cannot be fully utilized [3]. Because the reverse intersystem crossing rate
(
k RISC
)
constant is smaller than the radiative transition rate constant
(
k r
)
of the S1 to S0 state, the efficiency limitation of spin counter-count modulation during luminescence of thermally activated delayed fluorescence guest materials can be overcome [4]. As a result, in addition to the ns level's prompt lifetime, the delayed lifetime of the μs or even ms can be observed [5–8]. To suppress the aggregation concentration-induced quenching (ACQ) and triplet-triplet annihilation (TTA) problems of the guest in current research, it is usually doped into CBP, mCBP, mCP, DPEPO and other common host materials to get higher photoluminescence quantum yield (
Φ PL
), but additional attention needs to be paid to energy level matching in the selection of host materials to achieve energy transfer between host and guest [9–12]. It is well known that the
Φ PL
is highly dependent on molecular rigidity [13,14]. Many studies are currently underway to improve the performance of TADF by incorporating Cu (I) and Ag (I) into organic molecules to form complexes [13,15–22]. However, because of the overlap of the excitation and ground state vibrational wave functions, this twist increases the nonradiative relaxation rate, however, the final
Φ PL
is low, and many are less than 10 %, some are even less than 1 % [13,14,21,23,24]. As a result, it is critical to find a suitable carrier material to improve the
Φ PL
of TADF powder in the atmospheric environment while also achieving stability.

It is well known that when the energy difference between the S1 state and the T1 states is small to a certain extent (0.1 eV), efficient TADF luminescence and higher PLQY can be achieved through a small ΔEST, and the radiation rate between the S1 state and the S0 state can be increased, resulting in high luminous efficiency [25]. To produce a lower ΔEST in TADF materials, the dihedral angle between the donor and acceptor parts must be near to 90°. Simultaneously, in order to ensure high fluorescence emission, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the frontier molecular orbitals must overlap to keep the molecule constant. The effective radiative transition's stiffness is determined. The presence of –CN makes the stokes shift smaller, and the molecular relaxation is restricted during the excitation of the S0–S1 state. The presence of –CN makes the recombination energy smaller and suppresses the non-radiative relaxation from the T1-S1 state. Because of the presence of –CN, the dihedral angle between donor and acceptor is reduced, and nonradiative relaxation is reduced [26]. As a strong acceptor unit, the dihedral angle may overlap to some amount and has made significant progress in the field of OLED [27]. To generate TADF guest molecules, dicyanobenzene was used as a strong acceptor to interact with typical donors 3,6-di-tert-butylcarbazole and 3,6-diphenylcarbazole units.

It is worth mentioning that the sensitivity of the triplet state to oxygen frequently leads to quenching, which eventually lowers the luminous efficiency and stability in the atmospheric environment [28–30]. The present triplet state preservation method focuses on making neat films and devices by encapsulation to minimize interaction with oxygen, but the cost is high and material universality cannot be attained. Of course, additional uses exist, such as the separation of oxygen and moisture by nanoparticle encapsulation to accomplish applications in chemical sensing and biological imaging, but the tedious methods restrict its large-scale implementation [31–35]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with polymer chains and crosslinked polymer networks can induce room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), and highly crosslinked systems and a large number of energy levels can limit the vibration and rotation of subluminophores such as CO, CN and PO, reduce non-radiative relaxation, and achieve cross-linking enhancement emission (CEE) [36–40]. As a result, this work combines the properties of CPDs to composite TADF materials, employing a Si–O network to effectively stabilize the triplet state of TADF, constrain the vibration and rotation of the triplet state, realize efficient energy transfer between CPDs and TADF, and TADF materials in the full range are luminous stable, resistant to strong oxidants, and have good water repellency. Finally, the TADF molecules and TADF@CPDs synthesized in this study were used in ECL signal detection, demonstrating the multifunctionalization of TADF materials and presenting a specific notion for material design in the field of biosensing.

Scheme 1 depicts synthetic routes to Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz. The first material, Ph-1 (3-amino-3-phenylacrylonitrile) was obtained by nucleophilic addition reaction. Then Ph-1-Br (4-(4-bromophenyl)-2, 6-diphenylpyridine-3, 5-dicarbonitrile) was produced by Friedel-Crafts acylation with Ph-1 and 4-bromobenzaldehyde to provide an intermediate with an 85.0 % yield, which was then coupled with 3, 6-di-tert-butyl-9H-carbazole or 3, 6-diphenyl-9H-carbazole under basic conditions to yield Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz. The chemical structure of the target molecules was validated by their 1H NMR,13C NMR, high-resolution MS spectra.

In order to obtain the ground state characteristics of the molecule, calculations were performed under PBE0/6-31G (d, p) using density functional theory (DFT). As shown in Fig. 1 , the HOMO orbitals of the two guest molecules are mainly distributed in the donor 3, 6-di-tert-butylcarbazole or 3, 6-diphenylcarbazole and the adjacent benzene ring. Due to the electron-gaining nature of –CN, the LUMO of the two guest molecules are mainly distributed in the acceptor unit, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals are effectively separated to promote the RISC process, and the HOMO orbital extends to the adjacent benzene ring to enhance the molecular rigidity, the non-radiative relaxation is effectively suppressed, and the final twist angle between the CN-pyridine acceptor moiety and the benzene bridge is about 55°. The central N on the pyridine and H on the adjacent substituent groups appear close C–H⋯N (2.52 Å) distances, proving the existence of intramolecular interactions of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz. This interaction was demonstrated by the Interaction Region Indicator (IRI) method of the Multiwfn program [41], as shown in Fig. S1, it can be clearly seen that the occurrence between the N on the central pyridine and the H on the adjacent substituent groups show strong van der Waals interactions (green isosurfaces), make the molecules as a whole have a planar structure, and confinement structures can also be formed in these molecules, thereby suppressing the nonradiative relaxation path.

Singlet and triplet excitation energies were obtained by calculating the excited states under PBE0/6-31G (d, p) by time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT). As shown in Fig. 1, from the distribution of natural transition orbitals (NTOs), it is known that the S1 and T1 states of the three compounds show charge transfer (CT) characteristics. Taking Ph-tBuCz as an example, the holes of NTOs are mainly distributed in 3, 6-di-tert-butylcarbazole and the adjacent benzene ring, while the electrons of NTOs are mainly distributed on the acceptor unit, which finally makes the holes and electrons in NTOs get a small ΔEST (0.09 eV), which promotes TADF.

First, the photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra of the TADF guest powders were tested, as shown in Fig. 2 , Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz correspond to the emission (excitation) of 512 nm (341 nm) and 577 nm (466 nm), respectively. At this time, the Δstokes (71 nm) of Ph-DPCz is much smaller than that of Ph-tBuCz (111 nm), effectively reducing the non-radiative energy inactivation in solid state, and finally the
Φ PL
of Ph-DPCz is as high as 17.45 % in atmospheric environment. In order to simulate the real environment of the guest molecule, the non-polar toluene solvent was used to test its luminescence properties. The Δstokes of Ph-tBuCz in toluene solvent is reduced to 66 nm. Such a small Δstokes effectively avoids the deactivation of excitons in the form of non-radiative energy. Generally, the difference between the emission spectra of TADF in powder and toluene solution is about 20 nm, but For Ph-DPCz, the difference is close to 100 nm, and it is speculated that Ph-DPCz is more sensitive to solvents and has larger configurational distortion in organic solvents.

Based on this, the influence of different polar solvents on the guest molecules was explored. As shown in Fig. S2, with the increase of polarity, due to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect, both of them showed an obvious red shift relative to the toluene solution, which is attributed to the existence of the donor and the acceptor to make it have a charge transfer effect. With the increase of the solvent, the energy of the ground state (absorption spectrum) changes little or does not change, while the fluorescence spectrum emitted by the excited state relaxation changes, resulting in a redshift. However, the fluorescence intensity of Ph-DPCz is greatly reduced, indicating that its molecular dipole moment is much larger than that of Ph-tBuCz, and its interaction with solvent molecules is stronger, resulting in a greater loss of non-radiative energy and a more severe decrease in fluorescence intensity [42–45]. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, a strong localized π–π* transition sharp peak is displayed around 300 nm. Since the electron donating ability of 3, 6-di-tert-butylcarbazole is much greater than that of 3, 6-diphenylcarbazole, it is more obvious that the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) of Ph-tBuCz at 350–450 nm. Surprisingly, the PLQY of Ph-tBuCz in toluene solution with a concentration of 10−3-10−5 M still exceeds that of traditional TADF, and this material may exist in the form of dimer or exciplex in toluene solvent. Protecting it from environmental influences to take advantage of triplet excitons that are easily quenched by oxygen [46,47]. In addition, the results in Table 1 show that the PLQY results of Ph-tBuCz are far more ideal than those of Ph-DPCz, which may also be derived from the characteristics of the molecular structure itself. The existence of 3, 6-di-tert-butylcarbazole may promote the hole-electron is effectively transported and uniformly distributed, and the introduction of 3, 6-di-tert-butylcarbazole is usually considered in the design of TADF molecules to make the molecule highly soluble, which is convenient for the preparation of vacuum evaporation devices.

Since the display applications are still dominated by the solid state, TADF triplet excitons are stabilized by means of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). First, the optimal compounding ratio was screened out by setting different mass fraction ratios of TADF/CPDs through tentative experiments. The final optimized optimal ratios were 1:1 for Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and 1:5 for Ph-DPCz@CPDs, and the following tests were conducted with the components in the optimal compound ratio. As shown in Table 1, the PLQY of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs is 34.40 %, and the PLQY of Ph-DPCz@CPDs is 30.57 %. The PLQY of both exceeds that of traditional powder TADF, indicating that CPDs makes the triplet state participate effectively. Preliminary speculation is due to the introduction of Si–O network bonds in CPDs to reinforce the TADF guest and reduce triplet non-radiative deactivation. As shown in Fig. S3, Ph-tBuCz@CPDs brings a delayed lifetime of 8.90 μs in addition to the prompt lifetime of 6.22 ns, similarly Ph-DPCz@CPDs have both a prompt lifetime of 7.75 ns and a delayed of 7.51 μs. The existence of the two delayed lifetimes verifies the above conjecture, that is, the triplet state can effectively participate in the reverse intersystem crossing process in the presence of CPDs to achieve radiative luminescence.

In order to further prove that there is a certain interaction between TADF/CPDs to improve the luminescence properties, powder XRD test was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CPDs showed an amorphous structure, and some strong diffraction peaks of Ph-tBuCz disappear in Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and new characteristic peaks appear, indicating that there is a certain interaction between Ph-tBuCz and CPDs. For Ph-DPCz@CPDs, the proportion of TADF molecules is low, and CPDs mask the typical characteristic peaks of Ph-DPCz. Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs have an absorption band in the range of 300 nm–400 nm, which is mainly attributed to the n-π* transition of CO.

The structure of the complex was analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 4 , the dominant chemical bond of CPDs is the Si–O bond corresponding to three characteristic peaks, namely the Si–O–Si symmetric stretching vibration at 800 cm−1 and the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching vibration at 1107 cm−1, and Si–O bending vibration at 473 cm−1 [48]. Broadbands in the range of 840–1400 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si and Si–O–C [49,50]. The absorption bands of C–H, O–H and N–H at 3090-2775 cm−1, 3728-3213 cm−1 and 3528-3346 cm−1, respectively [51,52]. In addition, a strong stretching vibration of CO can be observed at 1638 cm−1, indicating that CO also contributes to the formation of CPDs [53]. The CO bond in Ph-tBuCz@CPDs shifts from 1638 cm−1 to 1604 cm−1 at short wavelength, which may be caused by the formation of hydrogen bonds between Ph-tBuCz and CPDs [54]. The XPS results are shown in Figs. S4–S6, CPDs include C, N, O, Si elements [49]. The C 1s in the XPS spectrum includes three main C–C (284.5 eV), C–N/C–O (285.7 eV), CO (287.7 eV) bonds [54]. N 1s includes only one component from the C–NH2 (399.7 eV) bond in ethylenediamine [55]. O 1s includes three main components of C–O (531.4 eV), Si–O (532.6 eV), and CO (533.2 eV) bonds [49,50]. Si 2p has only one main fitted peak originating from SiO2 (103.3 eV) [49].

It is worth noting that the N 1s of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs in the XPS spectra changed from a single peak of CPDs to three main peaks, except for C–NH2 (399.7 eV), PhCN (399.4 eV) corresponding to the nitrile group –CN from the TADF acceptor moiety, C–N (400.4 eV) from pyridine N. Comparing the binding energy positions of each functional group from the XPS results, it can be seen that the CO in C 1s and O 1s in Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs are 286.7 eV and 533.4 eV, respectively, which are both 286.7 eV and 533.4 eV respectively. The binding energy changes, the generation of CO bond may be due to the hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate to silicic acid and ethanol, and the ethanol is dissolved in the oxygen in the reaction solution to form CO component [36], combined with the FTIR results, it can be seen that there may be O−H⋯N hydrogen bonding interactions between TADF and CPDs, resulting in changes in FTIR and XPS spectra.

The CV and ECL phenomena of the materials were tested in PBS (7.43) solution and a three-electrode system with a platinum electrode as the working electrode, Pt wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 5 , a clear signal was observed at the anode during the ECL scan, with the anode starting position at +1.02 V for Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and the ECL reaching an intensity of 2892 a. u. at +1.42 V. The anode starting position was +0.83 V for Ph-tBuCz. The anodic starting point of +0.99 V and the highest luminescence intensity of 1773 a. u. corresponding to +1.42 V are clearly observed during the ECL annihilation of Ph-DPCz@CPDs, while the anodic starting point of Ph-DPCz is 0.83 V. The luminescence intensity of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs is lower than that of Ph-tBuCz and Ph-DPCz, which may be due to the highly interconnected network structure of CPDs hindering charge injection and transport [56–58]. In addition, it is clearly observed from the CV plots that the peak positions of the onset of electrochemical oxidation of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs (0.557 V) and Ph-DPCz@CPDs (0.588 V) are significantly different from those of Ph-tBuCz (0.461 V) and Ph-DPCz (0.482 V). Thus, it is clear from the anodic peak position, the onset of electrochemical oxidation in CV and the anodic signal that the reaction between CPDs and the co-reactant tripropylamine (TPrA) and the negatively charged CPDs
(
CPD
s
• −
)
are much more stable than the positively charged species
(
CPD
s
• +
)
in the electrochemical reduction process under PBS environment [59,60]. The possible reaction mechanism may be as follows: during the electrochemical oxidation process, first TPrA is deprotonated to generate strongly reducing
TPr
A •
, further increasing the electrochemical oxidation voltage, Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs are oxidized to
CPD
s
• +
through loss of electrons. Next, charge transfer processes occur in the generated
CPD
s
• +
and
TPr
A •
. Finally, ECL radioluminescence is achieved by generating excitons on CPDs through the deactivation pathway (e.g. TADF pathway) [35,60]. As shown in Fig. 5 of the test results, the ECL stability of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs is excellent, and the intensity of ECL hardly decreases after 25 turns of testing, which provides a certain reference value for conducting further applications in the field of biosensing.

Based on the above reaction phenomena and discussion of the results, it is speculated that the improved
Φ PL
and excellent stability of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs are attributed to the introduction of CPDs into the initial TADF guest molecule to accelerate the RISC process. In this work, the possible internal structure of CPDs is inferred by combining FTIR, XPS and the test results of related optical properties, as shown in Fig. S7. A large number of covalent and hydrogen bonds exist inside CPDs, which are distributed around the TADF molecule to form a highly cross-linked system to limit molecular vibration and rotation, inhibit molecular distortion, avoid non-radiative energy loss, and enhance molecular rigidity to promote radiation inactivation. CPDs possess a large number of energy levels that provide energy for the TADF jump from the T1*-T1 state, and lowering the jump energy barrier to reduce ΔEST promotes the RISC process. As demonstrated by testing the 300 K steady-state fluorescence spectra and 77 K phosphorescence spectra assisted by Fig. S7, the ΔEST of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs was 0.05 eV, which is significantly smaller compared to Ph-tBuCz (0.09 eV) and Ph-DPCz (0.16 eV), and the ΔEST of Ph-DPCz is slightly larger than that of Ph-tBuCz, because the molecular rigidity is stronger, and it is consistent with the trend of the theoretical calculation, so the above speculation is valid.

Molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) analysis is commonly used to explain molecular activity, hydrogen bonding interactions and chemical activity. The charge distribution is closely related to the molecular properties, so this work analyzes the relationship between intermolecular interactions and electronic activity of compounds by MESP. As shown in Fig. 6 , molecular van der Waals surface maps, intermolecular penetration maps, and electron density difference maps for electrostatic potential coloring are plotted using the Multiwfn program in combination with VMD analysis [61–63], with the blue-white-red color scale axis corresponding to increasing values of electrostatic potential, the blue region corresponding to the negative potential of electronegative atomic lone pairs of electrons, the red region representing electron deficient properties and partial positive charge, and the white region indicates the neutral potential. In chemical reactions molecules are always attracted to each other by electrostatic gravity, as shown in Fig. 6, the –OH functional group in CPDs appears to have a positive potential of +49.82 kcal mol−1 at the extreme point (red region), showing an active nucleophilic attack characteristic. The blue region corresponding to the –CN functional group in Ph-tBuCz shows a negative potential with a very small value of −36.01 kcal mol−1, and the blue region corresponding to the –CN functional group in Ph-DPCz shows a negative potential with a very small value of −32.97 kcal mol−1, showing an active electrophilic attack feature. As shown in Fig. 6, the solid line represents the region of increasing electron density and the dashed line represents the region of decreasing electron density, following the above-mentioned extreme and minimal points, the CPDs approach toward Ph-tBuCz/Ph-DPCz, forming the O−H⋯N hydrogen bonding interaction region, and the formation of hydrogen bonding causes the electron-electron density difference diagram of H electron density of atoms decreases, and finally a hydrogen bonding network is formed through electrostatic potential attraction. The intermolecular penetration diagrams shown in Fig. 6, due to the complementary characteristics of electrostatic potentials that make the van der Waals surface red and blue interpenetrate, Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs exhibit interpenetration distances of 3.02 Å and 3.14 Å, respectively. It shows that the hydrogen bond between the two is essentially caused by electrostatic attraction.

To further investigate the stability of the complex, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), solvent-dependent-photoluminescence intensity, pH-photoluminescence intensity, strong oxidant (H2O2) stability, and water stability analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. S8, the temperature of Ph-tBuCz@CPDs corresponding to 5 % weight loss is 381.3 °C, which is higher than the Td value of the powder state, indicating that the composite temperature forms a more stable structure after the introduction of appropriate hydrogen bonds. Obviously, a higher thermal decomposition temperature also appeared in Ph-DPCz@CPDs than powder Ph-DPCz, indicating that this composite method can effectively improve the thermal stability of TADF materials. Fig. S9 shows the effect of different polar solvents on the luminescence intensity of the complexes. It is gratifying that Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs can still maintain a certain fluorescence under the condition of increasing solvent polarity. The intensity and sensitivity to solvents are lower than their respective powder states, which fully indicates that the introduction of CPDs provides a shelter for the TADF guest molecules to avoid the attack of polar solvents and damage the internal structure, thereby enhancing the luminescence. In order to enhance the universality of the material, it was placed in the full pH range for detection, as shown in Fig. 7 , both Ph-tBuCz@CPDs and Ph-DPCz@CPDs showed extremely high stability from the strong acid-strong base range, not only the luminescence intensity is basically unchanged, but also the fluorescence emission peak does not change with the change of pH. As shown in Fig. S10, when the material was placed in a strong oxidant 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution after 0 days, 30 days and 60 days, the corresponding fluorescence intensity did not decay significantly. Even if it was placed in tap water, there are only minor changes after 60 days, so the two composite materials provide a more meaningful reference value for applications in severe cases.

Thermally activated delayed fluorescence achieves nearly 100 % exciton utilization from T1 to S1 state, but its triplet excitons are easily quenched by oxygen quenching factors, resulting in limited exciton utilization in atmospheric environments. In this work, the rationality of designing TADF molecules was first predicted by theoretical calculations of electronic structure and interaction region indicator (IRI), and secondly, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were introduced to stabilize the triplet state of TADF and reduce nonradiative relaxation. Finally, through experiments, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) analysis and electron density difference maps, it is proved that the luminescence mechanism between CPDs and TADF is due to the formation of O−H⋯N hydrogen bond interactions, which promote the reverse intersystem cross channeling of thermally activated delayed fluorescence. Realizing high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of powder state in air, solve the problem of stability of TADF in air, strong acid, strong base, peroxide and water, and apply this material on ECL to achieve effective ECL signal detection. It provides a certain reference for the expansion and application of TADF materials in the biological field in the future.

Jian Wang (First and Corresponding Author): Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Ling Chen: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft Preparation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
